- **FedAgentKE** introduces a lightweight framework for **Federated Semantic Knowledge Evolution**, bridging the gap between isolated, heterogeneous LLM agent frameworks.
- The system employs **Iterative Semantic Knowledge Distillation** to extract high-level reasoning abstractions from local agent trajectories while maintaining data privacy.
- A centralized **Aggregation and Adaptation** mechanism synthesizes these fragments into a global knowledge base that can be deployed back to individual agents.
- The architecture facilitates **cross-framework knowledge transfer**, allowing agents with different underlying models or tools to benefit from collective reasoning experiences.
key_results: Achieved consistent performance gains in cross-framework agent interaction scenarios.
Demonstrated successful transfer of reasoning logic across diverse task domains.
Ensured privacy-preserving knowledge sharing by avoiding raw trajectory exchange.
Validated scalability for heterogeneous agent ecosystems with varying model architectures.
Improved individual agent efficiency through adaptation of global semantic abstractions.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce FedAgentKE to enable heterogeneous AI agents to collaboratively evolve reasoning capabilities without sharing raw data.

title: Toward Federated Cognitive Digital Twins over the Edge-to-Cloud Continuum
summary: Digital Twins (DTs) are increasingly adopted to monitor, analyze, and optimize Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) through continuous interaction between physical assets and their digital counterparts. However, current DT architectures often rely on centralized and monolithic designs, leading to scalability, latency, and resilience issues in distributed environment such as smart cities. Moreover, they provide limited support for semantic integration and high-level reasoning, reducing the effectiveness of DT-based decision-making. Recent studies on Federated Digital Twins (FDTs) have addressed scalability by decomposing complex systems into interacting twins, but they still largely centralize intelligence in cloud components. In parallel, Cognitive Digital Twins (CDTs) enhance DTs with semantic reasoning, explainability, and AI-driven decision support, yet they are typically difficult to integrate into distributed architectures. This paper proposes a Federated Cognitive Digital Twin (FCDT) architecture that combines federation and cognition within a unified approach. The architecture distributes intelligence across the edge-to-cloud continuum through local twins, which provide real-time monitoring and lightweight cognitive capabilities, and global twins, which perform system-level reasoning, simulation, and coordination. By integrating distributed autonomy with cognitive reasoning, the proposed approach improves scalability, responsiveness, and decision-making in complex distributed CPSs

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **Federated Cognitive Digital Twin (FCDT)** architecture that bridges the gap between decentralized scalability and centralized intelligence.
- Distributes **lightweight cognitive capabilities** to the edge for real-time monitoring while utilizing the cloud for system-level reasoning and simulation.
- Enhances **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs)** by integrating semantic reasoning and explainable AI-driven decision support within a federated framework.
- Resolves traditional **latency and resilience** issues by moving intelligence closer to the data source across the edge-to-cloud continuum.
key_results: Development of a unified architecture combining federation and cognitive reasoning for distributed systems.
Implementation of local twins at the edge for real-time, low-latency monitoring and autonomy.
Utilization of global twins for high-level coordination and long-term system simulation.
Improved semantic integration to support complex decision-making in smart city environments.

processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a Federated Cognitive Digital Twin architecture to distribute semantic reasoning and coordination across edge-cloud networks.

title: Embodied inquiry with AI as facilitator: an exploratory case study
summary: Generative AI is entering science education at a time when the embodied education community is asking what such systems cannot do. Rather than asking whether AI can understand the body, this exploratory case study asks where a language-based AI can stand within an inquiry activity without displacing embodied experience. University students in a Master's course in physics education investigated the statics of fluids following the ISLE approach (Investigative Science Learning Environment) in a two-phase design: students first built the buoyancy model with their own hands, without AI; a purpose-configured AI assistant then facilitated the application of the model to a new phenomenon. We discuss what a language-based facilitator cannot reach and the value of a design in which AI complements embodied inquiry rather than replacing it.

processed.summary:
- Explores the integration of **Generative AI** as a facilitator within the **ISLE (Investigative Science Learning Environment)** framework for physics education.
- Employs a **two-phase pedagogical architecture** that prioritizes physical manual experimentation before introducing a **purpose-configured AI assistant**.
- Investigates the limitations of **language-based models** in capturing or replacing the sensory-motor **embodied experience** of scientific inquiry.
key_results: AI serves as a bridge between manual data collection and abstract theoretical application.
Direct physical interaction remains essential for grounding scientific concepts in reality.
Purpose-configured assistants provide scaffolding without reducing the necessary cognitive load of inquiry.
Embodied experiences create a conceptual foundation that AI propositional logic cannot replicate.
The hybrid approach maintains student agency while leveraging AI for complex reasoning and model application.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University researchers show that AI facilitators enhance physics learning by scaffolding inquiry without displacing essential physical embodied experiences.

title: Quality-Aware Multimodal Fusion Reveals Implicit Identity in Valence-Arousal Features
summary: Conventional face recognition relies on static appearance cues and degrades in unconstrained settings with expression variation, occlusion, and poor lighting. We hypothesize that audiovisual expression dynamics carry identity-discriminative information complementary to static appearance, and that extracting this signal requires multimodal representations robust to the variable input quality of in-the-wild video. To learn such representations, we cast multimodal valence-arousal (VA) estimation as a pretext task and propose Quality-Aware Adaptive Fusion (QAAF), which estimates per-sample, per-modality reliability and adapts each modality's contribution through learned soft gating and a quality-dependent dropout. For the problem of VA estimation, QAAF achieves an average Concordance Correlation Coefficient (CCC) of 0.472 via late fusion ensembling on Aff-wild2, improving over a baseline ensemble under the same setting (0.415) as well as a single-backbone baseline (0.288). Furthermore, the proposed QAAF demonstrates greater resilience to unavailable modalities, with only a 7.5-34.4% relative decrease in CCC when one modality is missing. We then probe whether these VA-trained features encode identity without identity-specific training. On AFEW-VA (67 actors) and YTF (1,595 subjects), VA-trained backbone features rank first among evaluated soft biometric methods, and score-level fusion with ArcFace lowers EER on both datasets (0.022 to 0.021 on AFEW-VA, 0.106 to 0.104 on YTF), correcting 68.2% of ArcFace's false accepts on AFEW-VA. These findings establish multimodal VA estimation as a soft biometric modality complementary to conventional face recognition.

processed.summary:
* Proposes **Quality-Aware Adaptive Fusion (QAAF)**, a framework that estimates per-modality reliability to dynamically weight audiovisual contributions via **learned soft gating**. 
* Utilizes **Valence-Arousal (VA) estimation** as a pretext task to capture identity-discriminative expression dynamics that are often missed by static appearance models. 
* Implements **quality-dependent dropout** to maintain system robustness, showing significantly lower performance degradation when one modality is completely unavailable. 
* Validates VA-trained features as a **soft biometric** that complements traditional face recognition systems like **ArcFace** by correcting specific failure cases.
key_results: Achieved an average CCC of 0.472 on Aff-wild2, outperforming the single-backbone baseline of 0.288.
Maintained performance with only a 7.5-34.4% relative CCC decrease when one modality was missing.
VA-trained features ranked first among evaluated soft biometric methods on AFEW-VA and YTF datasets.
Score-level fusion with ArcFace improved EER from 0.022 to 0.021 on the AFEW-VA dataset.
Corrected 68.2% of ArcFace's false acceptance errors on the AFEW-VA benchmark.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that multimodal valence-arousal features provide robust soft biometric signals that significantly enhance conventional face recognition accuracy.

title: Regulating autonomous and agentic AI
summary: Regulating activities where regulatees use autonomous and agentic AI is challenging. Regulatory assumptions about regulatee knowledge and control no longer hold true; much of that lies elsewhere in the AI supply chain which thus needs to be brought within the scope of regulation. Governance systems for autonomous AI cannot replicate existing governance models, but need a fresh approach. Retrospective supervisory oversight becomes ineffective as a risk management tool, and AI autonomy generates new systemic risks which require new solutions. This paper investigate four regulatory systems: UK regulation of content platforms, data protection, UK financial services, and the EU AI Act\'92s cross-sectoral regime. It analyses the challenges posed by autonomous and agentic AI and proposes potential solutions which regulators might adopt. These will transform regulation from a reactive process to an active one, and assist it in adapting to the challenges of AI autonomy.

processed.summary:
* The study addresses the breakdown of traditional regulatory frameworks when applied to **autonomous and agentic AI**, where control and technical knowledge shift from the end-user to the **AI supply chain**.
* It critiques the reliance on **retrospective supervisory oversight**, arguing that the non-deterministic nature of agents requires a shift toward **active monitoring** and real-time risk management.
* The research evaluates existing regimes such as the **EU AI Act** and UK financial services to propose solutions for managing the **systemic risks** inherent in autonomous decision-making loops.
key_results: Identification of 'control gaps' where end-users lack the ability to intervene in agentic workflows.
Evidence that retrospective audits are insufficient for mitigating real-time autonomous risks.
Proposed shift of regulatory liability upstream toward AI developers and foundation model providers.
Requirement for new governance models that account for the non-linear behavior of multi-agent systems.
Strategic roadmap for transforming regulation from a reactive process into an active technical gatekeeping role.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers advocate for shifting AI regulation from reactive oversight to active supply-chain monitoring to mitigate agentic autonomy risks.

title: M$^3$-Gen: Interpretable Multimodal Generation of Gene Expression Profiles Using Clinical and Imaging Data
summary: Integrating heterogeneous biomedical data, including clinical metadata, histopathology images, and molecular profiles, is crucial for comprehensive disease understanding. However, gene expression data acquisition remains constrained by high costs and privacy concerns, limiting its use in multimodal research and AI-driven applications. We present MultiModal Molecular Generation (M$^3$-Gen), a novel framework for the generation of gene expression profiles by conditioning a Generative Adversarial Network on histopathology images and clinical metadata. M$^3$-Gen learns a unified latent representation from the clinical variables and the images, leveraging contrastive learning, and exploits the embeddings of the two modalities to guide a generative model in producing biologically coherent gene expression profiles. Evaluations on the TCGA dataset demonstrate that M$^3$-Gen generates realistic and functionally meaningful gene expression data. Importantly, by integrating multiple modalities in an attention-based mechanism, M$^3$-Gen provides intrinsic explainability: it allows the identification of which regions of the histopathology images most strongly influenced the generation of specific gene expression profiles, making the model's decisions interpretable by design.

processed.summary:
- M3-Gen utilizes a **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** architecture to synthesize high-dimensional gene expression profiles conditioned on histopathology images and clinical metadata.
- The framework employs **contrastive learning** to align disparate modalities into a unified latent representation, ensuring the generated profiles are biologically coherent.
- The integration of an **attention-based mechanism** enables intrinsic interpretability, allowing researchers to map generated molecular data to specific spatial regions in tissue images.
- The model addresses the high cost and privacy constraints of molecular data acquisition by providing a **generative pathway** for synthetic biomedical data.
key_results: Successfully synthesized realistic gene expression profiles using the TCGA dataset.
Demonstrated functional coherence between generated profiles and real-world biological data.
Achieved effective latent space alignment between clinical metadata and imaging via contrastive loss.
Enabled spatial explainability by identifying image regions influencing specific gene generation.
Validated that multimodal conditioning outperforms single-modality generation in biological accuracy.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce M3-Gen, a multimodal GAN that synthesizes interpretable gene expression profiles from clinical and histopathology data.

title: Capital Markets LLM Reliability Score (CM-LRS): From Plausible to Bankable
summary:
In capital-markets workflows the question is rarely whether a large language model can produce a fluent draft, but whether the draft is bankable: defensible in front of a counter-party or a regulator, with the documents in hand. Existing methods address parts of that gap: open-domain QA benchmarks reward surface accuracy, and finance benchmarks (FinanceBench, FinQA, ConvFinQA) advance document-grounded and numerical QA but evaluate at the question-answer layer rather than the workflow outputs practitioners defend.
  We introduce CM-LRS, a Capital Markets LLM Reliability Score, evaluating outputs at the workflow-output layer across seven dimensions: factual accuracy, evidence traceability, numerical consistency, workflow completeness, source discipline, decision usefulness, and reviewability/auditability. Each is scored 0-5 against a rubric anchored on signals reviewers in regulated settings use; the aggregate is tunable to the workflow.
  We demonstrate CM-LRS on five workflows (DCM transaction-terms extraction, precedent retrieval, issuer profile synthesis, M&A transaction-comparable reasoning, ECM transaction-terms extraction) over public SEC EDGAR filings, a public UK takeover release, and fictional synthetic supplements, scoring four models against four independent LLM judges spanning three model families.
  Three findings. First, the frontier closed-source models cluster within 0.22 points on four-judge averaged CM-LRS (Sonnet 4.6 = 4.31, Opus 4.7 = 4.30, GPT-5.5 = 4.09); all four judges place the open-weights baseline (Llama 3.3 70B = 3.15) last. Second, that gap concentrates on retrieval (2.23) and synthesis (2.15), not extraction (0.84). Third, Decision Usefulness shows the widest cross-model dispersion of any dimension (4.0 points on issuer profiling) and top-tier inter-judge agreement (mean r = 0.52).
  Plausibility is cheap. Bankability is the bar.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **CM-LRS**, a specialized evaluation framework for capital markets that prioritizes **bankable outputs** over surface-level fluency, focusing on regulatory and audit-ready workflows.
• Implements a **multidimensional rubric** spanning seven critical axes, including **evidence traceability**, numerical consistency, and **decision usefulness**, to assess model reliability in high-stakes environments.
• Employs an **LLM-as-a-judge** architecture to benchmark frontier models against five real-world financial workflows such as **M&A transaction-comparable reasoning** and DCM extraction.
key_results: Frontier closed-source models (Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5) clustered closely within 0.22 points on the reliability score.
Llama 3.3 70B (open-weights) significantly underperformed frontier models, particularly in retrieval and synthesis tasks.
The performance gap is largest in retrieval (2.23) and synthesis (2.15), but much narrower in extraction tasks (0.84).
Decision Usefulness is the most variable dimension across models, showing a 4.0 point dispersion in issuer profiling tasks.
The framework achieved high inter-judge agreement (mean r = 0.52) for subjective dimensions like auditability and usefulness.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CM-LRS to measure LLM bankability, finding closed-source models significantly lead in complex financial retrieval and synthesis.

title: SHIFT: Self-reconstruction Harnesses Implicit Fine-grained Thinking for Retrieval
summary: LLM-based retrievers have become a fundamental component of modern information retrieval systems. The paradigm of "rewrite-then-retriev" introduces explicit reasoning before retrieval. In addition, implicit-reasoning retrievers such as GIRCSE and LaSER improve efficiency by replacing explicit reasoning with soft tokens. Although these methods demonstrated competitive performance on reasoning-intensive retrieval benchmarks, they struggle to address the mismatch between the objectives of retrieval and generation. In this work, we propose SHIFT ($\textbf{S}$elf-reconstruction $\textbf{H}$arnesses $\textbf{I}$mplicit $\textbf{F}$ine-grained $\textbf{T}$hinking for Retrieval), a retrieval training framework based on LLMs. Firstly, we transfer LLMs into reasoning-efficient retrievers with residual projection and task-oriented bidirectional attention aggregation in the latent space. Secondly, we alleviate the mismatch between contrastive learning and implicit reasoning using fine-grained next-token-prediction-based reconstruction. Extensive experiments on reasoning-intensive retrieval benchmarks show that SHIFT consistently outperforms other widely used retrievers. We also carried out a detailed analysis to illustrate how our method works.

processed.summary:
- SHIFT transforms LLMs into **reasoning-efficient retrievers** by employing **residual projection** and task-oriented bidirectional attention aggregation in the latent space.
- The framework bridges the gap between **contrastive learning** and **implicit reasoning** by introducing a **fine-grained next-token-prediction-based reconstruction** loss.
- It addresses the objective mismatch in traditional retrievers by forcing the model to capture detailed semantics through **self-reconstruction** while maintaining retrieval efficiency.
- SHIFT demonstrates superior performance on **reasoning-intensive benchmarks** compared to explicit reasoning methods and existing soft-token implicit retrievers.
key_results: Consistently outperforms widely used retrievers like GIRCSE and LaSER.
Successfully alleviates the training mismatch between generation and retrieval objectives.
Enables efficient reasoning-intensive retrieval without the latency of 'rewrite-then-retrieve' paradigms.
Validates that latent space bidirectional attention aggregation improves semantic density.
Proves that fine-grained reconstruction enhances the model's ability to handle complex query-document reasoning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop SHIFT to improve LLM retrieval by using self-reconstruction for efficient, implicit reasoning-based embeddings.

title: Ensemble Logic for Symbolic Representation of Sleep Medicine Guidelines
summary: The American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM) Manual is the clinical standard for polysomnography (PSG) scoring, but its narrative rules can admit multiple reasonable interpretations, contributing to inter-scorer variability and implementation differences across studies and software systems. We present a formal framework for translating sleep-scoring rules into Rational Ensemble Logic (QEL), a dense-time (i.e., a continuous, rational-valued timeline rather than discrete steps) formalism that combines first-order quantification with metric temporal operators. Using an extraction-and-compilation procedure, we identified 18 unique atomic propositions and derived 12 final specifications corresponding to clinically scoreable AASM events. Back-translation of QEL specifications into clinician-facing language retained high semantic fidelity to the original scoring narratives (embedding cosine similarity: 79.3, 95% CI: 79.0--79.7) despite low lexical overlap (ROUGE-L: 18.3, 95 CI: 17.6--18.9). Formalization also clarifies latent ambiguities, including implicit physiological latencies and overlapping exclusions. This framework yields executable, rigorous rule specifications for computational phenotyping, more consistent implementation across datasets, and standardized open-source PSG analysis. This work is a part of the "Symbolic Biomedicine" program championed by the corresponding author.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **Rational Ensemble Logic (QEL)**, a dense-time symbolic formalism that translates narrative clinical guidelines into executable computational rules.
* Employs **first-order quantification** and metric temporal operators to handle the continuous, rational-valued timelines required for sleep medicine scoring.
* Leverages an **extraction-and-compilation procedure** to resolve latent ambiguities in medical text, such as overlapping exclusions and implicit physiological latencies.
* Facilitates standardized **computational phenotyping**, ensuring consistent implementation of clinical standards across diverse software systems and datasets.
key_results: Identified 18 unique atomic propositions and 12 final specifications for AASM events.
Achieved high semantic fidelity with a 79.3 embedding cosine similarity in back-translation.
Reported low lexical overlap (18.3 ROUGE-L), indicating the logic captures meaning over literal wording.
Successfully formalized clinical scoring rules into a dense-time continuous framework.
Established a methodology for the 'Symbolic Biomedicine' program to bridge narrative rules and automation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The Symbolic Biomedicine program introduces Rational Ensemble Logic to transform ambiguous clinical sleep guidelines into rigorous, executable symbolic representations.

title: From Static Bibliometrics to Dynamic Knowledge Graphs: An LLM-Powered Framework for Modernizing Science, Technology, and Innovation (STI) Analytics
summary: Bibliometric indicators - citation counts, h-indexes, co-authorship networks - have long anchored science, technology, and innovation (STI) analytics, yet suffer from temporal lag, semantic shallowness, and an inability to capture the non-linear dynamics of contemporary knowledge ecosystems. Dynamic knowledge graphs and large language models (LLMs) have each been proposed as remedies, but neither is sufficient alone: existing scholarly knowledge graphs remain largely static, while LLM-driven pipelines are prone to hallucination, opacity, and corpus bias without structured grounding. This paper proposes a hybrid, symbolic-first framework integrating all three traditions under explicit methodological constraint. Organized across five layers - an open scholarly data backbone, a dynamic versioned knowledge graph, a constrained LLM-assisted semantic augmentation layer, a multi-layer validation pipeline, and an analytics layer - the framework positions LLMs strictly as generators of provisional candidate enrichments. Candidates become analytically admissible only after passing structural, evidentiary, comparative, and selective expert validation, with full provenance recorded at every stage. The analytics layer supports both established bibliometric indicators and extended graph-based analyses, including trend emergence detection, science-to-technology pathway mapping, and policy-oriented gap analysis. The framework's central theoretical contribution is treating validation as the mediating principle between semantic flexibility and epistemic discipline, enabling STI analytics that is semantically richer and temporally more responsive than static bibliometrics while remaining aligned with the evidentiary standards of science-of-science research. Governance considerations addressing reproducibility, bias, and auditability are also discussed.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **hybrid symbolic-first architecture** that integrates open scholarly data with a **dynamic versioned knowledge graph** to overcome the temporal lag of traditional bibliometrics.
- Utilizes LLMs for **constrained semantic augmentation**, treating model outputs as provisional candidates that must pass a rigorous **multi-layer validation pipeline** (structural, evidentiary, and expert).
- Enables advanced analytics such as **trend emergence detection** and science-to-technology pathway mapping while maintaining full provenance for **auditability and reproducibility**.
- Addresses LLM limitations like hallucination and bias by grounding generative processes in **structured knowledge graphs** and explicit methodological constraints.
key_results: Integration of LLMs as generators of provisional candidate enrichments rather than authoritative sources.
Development of a five-layer framework: Data, Knowledge Graph, Augmentation, Validation, and Analytics.
Implementation of multi-layer validation including structural, evidentiary, and comparative checks.
Capability to map non-linear dynamics in science-to-technology pathways.
Framework for balancing semantic flexibility with strict epistemic discipline in STI research.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a hybrid LLM-Knowledge Graph framework to modernize science analytics through rigorous validation and semantic augmentation.

title: Toward cryptographically verifiable authorization for autonomous AI agents: A security hypothesis, preliminary formal model, and proof-of-concept implementation
summary: Autonomous AI agents increasingly execute actions, invoke tools, and operate on protected resources with limited human oversight. Existing authentication and authorization mechanisms establish identity and delegate authority, but do not inherently provide cryptographic evidence that a concrete request issued by a specific agent satisfies the applicable policy in a specific execution context. This paper hypothesizes that agent authorization can be formalized as a cryptographically verifiable relation, denoted $R_{CVA}$, that jointly binds an agent principal, a concrete authorization request, an execution context, and the satisfaction of an applicable policy, while selectively preserving the confidentiality of private authorization attributes. We introduce a preliminary formal abstraction for Cryptographically Verifiable Agent Authorization (CVA), define a compact set of candidate security properties including authorization soundness, principal binding, request binding, policy binding, and replay resistance, and provide an executable zero-knowledge proof of concept that instantiates selected elements of the model over a Groth16 zk-SNARK construction. We further identify and formalize the structural separation among identity binding, authorization-request binding, and runtime execution binding as a central open problem in the design of secure agentic systems (a distinction {not explicitly addressed by} current agentic security frameworks) and present a falsifiable research agenda for its resolution.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **Cryptographically Verifiable Agent Authorization (CVA)** framework to bridge the gap between agent identity and actual policy compliance during execution.
- Utilizes **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (zk-SNARKs)**, specifically the Groth16 construction, to verify authorization requests without exposing private attributes.
- Defines a rigorous security model focusing on **principal, request, and policy binding** to prevent unauthorized tool use or resource access.
- Highlights a critical research gap in **runtime execution binding**, which current agentic security frameworks fail to address adequately.
key_results: Formalized the R_CVA relation to bind agent identities to specific authorization requests and execution contexts.
Established core security properties including authorization soundness, principal binding, and replay resistance.
Implemented a functional proof-of-concept using Groth16 zk-SNARKs for private attribute verification.
Identified the structural separation of identity and execution binding as a primary vulnerability in agentic systems.
Proposed a falsifiable research agenda to standardize cryptographically verifiable actions for autonomous agents.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a zk-SNARK based framework to provide cryptographically verifiable authorization for autonomous AI agent actions.

title: Post Completeness in Conditional Logic
summary: A logic is Post complete if it is consistent but has no consistent proper extensions. In this article, we systematically investigate the Post complete extensions of certain basic conditional logics. We identify all of the finitely many regular and normal Post complete conditional logics, and prove analogues of Makinson's embedding theorems. We also show that certain basic conditional logics have uncountably many Post complete extensions for which closure under some, but not necessarily all, rules peculiar to the conditional are relaxed. We reflect on what our results tell us about the structure of certain lattices of conditional logics and also draw some morals for multimodal logic.

processed.summary:
• This research investigates **Post completeness** within the framework of conditional logics, identifying the boundaries of consistent logical extensions.
• The authors classify the finite set of **regular and normal** Post complete conditional logics, establishing a definitive map of these formal systems.
• The work proves **embedding theorems** analogous to Makinson's, bridging the gap between specific conditional rules and general multimodal logic.
• It explores the structural complexity of **logical lattices**, demonstrating that certain systems possess uncountably many extensions under relaxed closure rules.
key_results: Identification of all finitely many regular and normal Post complete conditional logics.
Verification of Makinson-style embedding theorems within conditional logic contexts.
Discovery of uncountably many Post complete extensions when certain conditional rules are relaxed.
Delineation of the relationship between conditional logic rules and logical closure properties.
Characterization of the structural properties of lattices governing multimodal logical systems.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers classify Post complete conditional logics, revealing complex lattice structures and proving new embedding theorems for multimodal systems.

title: Scaling Up Formal Representation of Clinical Trial Protocols in Ensemble Logic Using LLMs: A Preliminary Study
summary: The reliance on unstructured free text for documenting clinical trial protocols creates a significant barrier to automated reasoning, cohort discovery, and trial simulation. The lack of formal structure obscures critical temporal phenotypes, such as dynamic eligibility criteria and event timing constraints. Although Temporal Ensemble Logic (TEL) offers an expressive framework for modeling these elements, manual encoding remains a prohibitive bottleneck. We introduce the CT-TEL workflow: a scalable pipeline leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) to translate narrative clinical protocols into TEL formulas. We applied CT-TEL to generate logical models for 23 real-world trials from ClinicalTrials.gov. We evaluated translation fidelity via a back-translation approach, using LLMs to convert TEL formulas back into natural language and measuring semantic similarity against source texts. The resulting semantic retention suggests that LLMs may offer a pathway for mapping informal protocols to computable logic, providing preliminary evidence toward scalable clinical trial emulation within the emerging "Symbolic Biomedicine" paradigm championed by the corresponding author.

processed.summary:
- Introduces the **CT-TEL workflow**, a scalable pipeline that leverages **Large Language Models (LLMs)** to translate unstructured clinical trial protocols into **Temporal Ensemble Logic (TEL)** formulas.
- Addresses the bottleneck of manual encoding by automating the extraction of **temporal phenotypes** and complex eligibility constraints from narrative text.
- Employs a **back-translation evaluation** method where LLMs convert logic back to natural language to verify semantic fidelity against the original source.
- Provides a foundation for **Symbolic Biomedicine**, enabling automated reasoning, cohort discovery, and digital trial simulation through computable representations.
key_results: Developed a scalable LLM-driven pipeline (CT-TEL) for formalizing narrative clinical protocols.
Successfully generated logical models for 23 real-world trials sourced from ClinicalTrials.gov.
Validated translation accuracy using a semantic similarity metric via LLM back-translation.
Captured complex temporal and dynamic eligibility criteria that are typically obscured in free-text documentation.
Demonstrated the feasibility of mapping informal protocols to computable logic for automated clinical reasoning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce the CT-TEL workflow to automate the translation of narrative clinical protocols into computable symbolic logic.

title: AI Assistants Overassist
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as tutors and thought partners, helping users reason through problems. While guidance from AI assistants can scaffold thinking and foster learning, such benefits depend on how they help--for instance, intervening too early or too frequently may hinder true learning and cognitive engagement. Yet how AI systems navigate intervention decisions during problem-solving remains poorly understood. Here, we introduce Int-Bench, a simulation-based benchmark for evaluating LLM interventions during learning. Int-Bench simulates a "student" solving a problem while a "teacher" monitors the student's reasoning and decides whether, when, and how to intervene. Across three domains--code debugging, mathematics, and brain teasers--we evaluate LLM teachers on the frequency and timing of interventions, as well as their impact on both immediate task success and generalization to new problems. We also compare LLMs to humans, finding that LLMs intervene more frequently and earlier than humans. Moreover, in contrast to humans, they tend to provide complete solutions rather than targeted hints. These findings suggest that current LLM assistants often optimize for short-term success rather than supporting the reasoning processes needed for deeper learning and long-term success.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Int-Bench**, a simulation-based benchmark designed to evaluate the frequency, timing, and quality of **LLM interventions** during pedagogical reasoning tasks.
- Utilizes a **Teacher-Student simulation loop** across code debugging, mathematics, and brain teasers to measure the impact of AI guidance on student performance.
- Highlights a critical architectural bias where LLMs prioritize **short-term task success** over the development of **long-term reasoning** and generalization skills.
key_results: LLM teachers intervene significantly more frequently than human tutors across all tested domains.
AI assistants tend to provide complete solutions rather than the scaffolded hints favored by humans.
Models intervene much earlier in the problem-solving process, often preempting the 'productive struggle' needed for learning.
Current AI guidance strategies optimize for immediate task completion but result in poor generalization to new problems.
Int-Bench identifies a major gap between human-like pedagogical reasoning and current LLM intervention logic.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that LLM assistants prioritize immediate task completion over long-term learning by over-intervening with full solutions.

title: Expert Behavior Prior Reinforcement Learning
summary: Behavior prior reinforcement learning (BPRL) has emerged as a promising paradigm to improve sample efficiency in online reinforcement learning (RL) by leveraging policy priors derived from offline demonstrations. However, most existing BPRL methods rely on static offline datasets, which often suffer from low data diversity and suboptimal trajectory quality. This reliance restricts the effectiveness of policy priors, hindering both policy exploitation and stability during online training. Consequently, agents are prone to inefficient exploration and unstable learning dynamics. To address these limitations, we deviate from existing offline pre-training methods and propose an Expert Behavior Prior (EBP) algorithm. Specifically, we introduce a Q-guided conditional variational autoencoder (Q-CVAE) that learns to generate expert policy priors directly from the online replay buffer. This enables the generation of high-value actions for guiding policy updates without relying on pre-collected expert trajectories. To further enhance policy exploitation, we propose an expert policy guidance (EPG) mechanism that selects expert actions from a generative support set, and we integrate a policy gradient correction (PGC) module to harmonize Q-guidance with expert supervision, promoting stable and consistent policy improvement. Extensive experiments conducted on robotic control (Gym, PyBullet) and industrial control (DMControl) benchmarks demonstrate that EBP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art online RL algorithms, achieving higher sample efficiency and more stable convergence.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Expert Behavior Prior (EBP)**, a novel algorithm designed to improve **sample efficiency** in online reinforcement learning by moving away from reliance on static offline datasets.
- Features a **Q-guided conditional variational autoencoder (Q-CVAE)** that identifies and generates high-value policy priors dynamically from the **online replay buffer**.
- Implements an **Expert Policy Guidance (EPG)** mechanism and a **Policy Gradient Correction (PGC)** module to harmonize generative expert supervision with standard Q-guidance for stable convergence.
key_results: Eliminates the need for pre-collected high-quality expert trajectories for behavior priors.
Significant improvement in sample efficiency compared to state-of-the-art online RL algorithms.
Achieves more stable learning dynamics through a policy gradient correction mechanism.
Demonstrated superior performance across robotic (Gym, PyBullet) and industrial (DMControl) benchmarks.
Successfully leverages generative support sets to guide policy updates towards high-value actions.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop EBP to boost RL sample efficiency by generating expert policy priors directly from online replay buffers.

title: Unlearning Under Imbalance: Benchmarking Fairness in Multimodal LLM Unlearning
summary: Machine unlearning has emerged as a tool for removing personal data from trained models to comply with recent AI regulations. To evaluate unlearning effectiveness in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), prior works fine-tune models on fictitious identities, simulating unlearning requests on subsets of these IDs, which are typically uniformly distributed. However, in realistic scenarios, people from different demographic groups may request to be unlearned at different frequencies, potentially altering the model's internal beliefs for these groups and leading to biased behaviors. To fill this gap, we propose FAIRGET, the first Visual Question Answering benchmark that evaluates unlearning under unbalanced, realistic, forget requests. These requests are designed to simulate multiple realistic scenarios, ranging from simple to challenging settings, that lead to biased unlearned models if fairness is not accounted for. Additionally, we propose FAUN, the first unlearning algorithm for MLLMs that forgets unlearning data while preserving model fairness. FAUN exploits a bias-aware activation steering mechanism to unlearn identities while accounting for the unbalanced nature of the forget data. Experiments on FAIRGET and the established FIUBench demonstrate our method's superiority both in unlearning quality and fairness.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **FAIRGET**, the first Visual Question Answering benchmark specifically designed to evaluate **Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) unlearning** under realistic, imbalanced demographic request frequencies.
• Proposes **FAUN**, a novel unlearning algorithm that utilizes a **bias-aware activation steering** mechanism to remove specific identities while maintaining model fairness across different groups.
• Addresses the critical gap where non-uniform unlearning requests lead to **biased internal representations** and skewed model behaviors in standard fine-tuning approaches.
• Demonstrates a technical framework for balancing **data privacy compliance** (e.g., GDPR) with model utility and demographic equity in generative agents.
key_results: Development of the FAIRGET benchmark to simulate realistic, challenging unlearning scenarios.
Introduction of FAUN, achieving superior unlearning quality compared to existing state-of-the-art methods.
Validation that naive unlearning methods significantly degrade fairness when requests are demographically imbalanced.
Demonstration that activation steering can decouple identity removal from general task performance.
Consistent performance improvements across both the new FAIRGET benchmark and the established FIUBench.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce FAIRGET and FAUN to benchmark and improve demographic fairness during multimodal LLM identity unlearning.

title: Adaptive Depth Sparse Framework: Similarity-Driven Resource Allocation for Pre-Trained LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong generation and reasoning performance, but the Transformer architecture incurs high inference cost. Existing acceleration methods often rely on task-specific fine-tuning or training from scratch, increasing adaptation cost and limiting cross-task usability. We present an Adaptive Depth Sparse Framework (AdaDSF) that converts off-the-shelf pre-trained LLMs into depth-sparse models without full retraining. Our key insight is that layers contribute unequally to representation transformation, characterized by the cosine similarity between layer input and output hidden states. Based on this, AdaDSF assigns layer-wise token retention ratios from similarity statistics, uses a lightweight router to select informative tokens at each layer, and introduces a feature-preserving alignment objective to match intermediate and final representations between sparse and dense models. On GPT-NeoX and Qwen2.5 over language modeling and commonsense reasoning, AdaDSF substantially reduces inference FLOPs while preserving performance close to dense counterparts. Under comparable sparsity, AdaDSF consistently yields smaller accuracy degradation than strong baselines including MoD, D-LLM, and DLO.

processed.summary:
- **AdaDSF** converts pre-trained LLMs into depth-sparse models by analyzing **cosine similarity** between input and output hidden states to identify layer redundancy.
- The framework implements a **lightweight router** for layer-wise token selection and a **feature-preserving alignment objective** to match dense model representations.
- It eliminates the need for full retraining or task-specific fine-tuning, allowing for the efficient adaptation of off-the-shelf models like **GPT-NeoX** and **Qwen2.5**.
- The architecture optimizes resource allocation by assigning token retention ratios based on similarity statistics, substantially reducing **inference FLOPs**.
key_results: Substantially reduces inference FLOPs while maintaining performance near dense model levels.
Outperforms existing sparsity baselines including Mixture-of-Depths (MoD), D-LLM, and DLO.
Demonstrates successful zero-shot and reasoning performance on GPT-NeoX and Qwen2.5.
Validates that input-output hidden state similarity is an effective metric for layer-wise importance.
Maintains structural integrity through alignment objectives that preserve intermediate feature representations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce AdaDSF to reduce LLM inference costs using similarity-driven token routing without full model retraining.

title: Multi-Task Learning for Heterogeneous Prediction from Video Game State with Transfer Learning
summary: Multi-task learning (MTL) is a promising approach for prediction tasks derived from video game state data, as modern game telemetry provides multiple related supervision signals from the same structured observations. We study whether a shared model trained jointly across tasks in team-based multiplayer games can improve generalization while reducing training and inference cost compared to specialized single-task models. We adapt a multimodal architecture for endpoint prediction to a general multi-task setting that combines rasterized vision inputs, global match context, and per-unit state information through an image encoder and attention-based interaction modeling. Experiments on a large proprietary World of Tanks dataset compare single-task and multi-task training, evaluate weighting strategies for mixed losses and conflicting gradients, and test pre-training/fine-tuning under limited target-data regimes. We also examine within-game transfer across game maps under structured environment shift.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **multimodal architecture** that integrates rasterized vision inputs, global match context, and per-unit state via an image encoder and **attention-based interaction modeling**.
- Evaluates **Multi-Task Learning (MTL)** strategies to leverage multiple supervision signals from structured game telemetry, aiming to outperform single-task models.
- Investigates advanced optimization techniques, including **gradient conflict mitigation** and loss weighting strategies, to handle heterogeneous prediction endpoints.
- Explores **transfer learning** and pre-training/fine-tuning workflows to improve model performance under significant environment shifts and limited target-data constraints.

Joint training significantly reduced cumulative training and inference costs compared to maintaining separate models.

Models demonstrated robust within-game transfer capabilities when subjected to structured environment shifts across different game maps.
